skill.name: naming-analyzer
skill.description: Review code names and suggest clearer variables, functions, classes, files, and conventions based on project context.
skill.summary: Analyze naming clarity, consistency, and convention fit across codebases.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many backticks as shell execution and one environment variable example. Review found these are Markdown documentation examples, not executable code. The only material concern is that the skill can guide broad file review or renaming workflows, so users should approve edits.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Backtick Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code and fenced examples. They describe naming conventions and sample reports, and I found no executable shell command path. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The evidence is in a Markdown-only SKILL.md file, and the surrounding lines are naming examples, not runnable scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around identifiers and examples. No command runner, shell invocation, or install-time execution is present.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Example Is Illustrative Documentation
low_findings.description: The process.env.API_URL reference appears in a naming example that labels apiUrl as an acceptable abbreviation. It does not transmit, log, or otherwise expose environment values. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.93. Confidence reasoning: The line is inside a fenced JavaScript example and has no surrounding operational code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access appears only as sample code in documentation. There is no executable file or network sink using the value.

low_findings.title: Broad Refactoring Guidance Can Lead To File Changes
low_findings.description: The skill asks to analyze files and directories and offers a refactoring script workflow. This is legitimate for a naming tool but should require user confirmation before bulk renames or generated scripts run. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for low filesystem workflow risk. Confidence: 0.64. Confidence reasoning: The instructions explicitly mention file and directory review plus script creation, but no executable script is bundled.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill can guide broad codebase inspection and renaming. The risk is limited because the file contains instructions only, not executable automation.

content.value_statement: Poor names make code harder to review, refactor, and maintain. This skill finds vague, misleading, or inconsistent names and suggests clearer alternatives.
actual_capabilities: Reviews variable, function, class, type, file, table, and API endpoint names.
Identifies vague, misleading, abbreviated, inconsistent, or overly long names.
Checks language conventions such as camelCase, snake_case, PascalCase, and UPPER_SNAKE_CASE.
Suggests clearer replacement names with reasons for each change.
Produces a structured naming analysis report with severity levels and priorities.
Can propose safe refactoring steps when users request broad renaming.
limitations: It depends on the provided code context and may miss names outside the inspected files.
It does not guarantee behavior-preserving renames without project tests and IDE refactoring support.
It may prefer local project conventions over general style guides when patterns conflict.
It provides recommendations, not automatic validation across every framework.

use_cases.title: Review Feature Code Before Merge
use_cases.description: Find vague names in a pull request before reviewers spend time on readability comments.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Naming Rules
use_cases.description: Compare current code against team conventions and create a practical naming cleanup list.

use_cases.title: Clean Up Legacy Modules
use_cases.description: Prioritize misleading names, unclear abbreviations, and inconsistent boolean properties in older code.

prompt_templates.title: Review One File
prompt_templates.prompt: Use naming-analyzer to review this file. List unclear names, explain each issue, and suggest better names.

prompt_templates.title: Check Project Conventions
prompt_templates.prompt: Use naming-analyzer on this folder. Identify naming convention mismatches and group them by language or framework.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Rename Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use naming-analyzer to create a prioritized rename plan. Separate behavior-risky names from simple clarity improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Public API Names
prompt_templates.prompt: Use naming-analyzer to review exported APIs, database names, and endpoints. Flag names that could mislead users or clients.

output_examples.input: A service file contains vague names such as data, temp, process, and getUser with side effects.
output: Critical: getUser is misleading because it updates login data. Suggested name: fetchAndUpdateUserLogin.
Major: data hides the domain meaning. Suggested name: userProfile or apiResponse, depending on context.
Minor: temp is unclear outside a short local calculation. Suggested name: previousValue.

output_examples.input: A JavaScript module mixes API_url, active, MAX, and strToNum.
output: Convention issue: API_url mixes casing styles. Use API_URL for constants or apiUrl for variables.
Boolean issue: active should read as a state. Suggested name: isActive.
Clarity issue: strToNum is over-abbreviated. Suggested name: stringToNumber.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent boolean names across React components and backend models.
output: Use is for state, has for possession, can for ability, and should for decisions.
Rename ambiguous status and flag fields only after checking behavior and tests.
Document accepted abbreviations such as API, HTML, URL, and ID.

best_practices: Provide enough surrounding code for the skill to infer behavior and domain meaning.
Review suggested renames with project conventions before applying them broadly.
Use IDE rename tools and tests for changes that affect many references.
anti_patterns: Do not apply every suggested rename without checking public APIs or database contracts.
Do not use the skill on isolated identifiers when behavior context is required.
Do not replace accepted team abbreviations just to make names longer.

faq.question: What does this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It reviews names for variables, functions, classes, files, database fields, and API endpoints.

faq.question: Can it rename code automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest rename plans, but users should approve edits and use safe refactoring tools.

faq.question: Does it support multiple languages?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions cover JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, and Go conventions.

faq.question: How does it rank problems?
faq.answer: It separates misleading names, vague names, convention violations, and lower priority cleanup items.

faq.question: Can it follow project-specific naming rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It asks the assistant to consider existing project patterns before recommending changes.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive codebases?
faq.answer: Use normal code review safeguards. The skill has no bundled executable code, but it may inspect provided files.
